_tier_note: raw source_tier values here are non-conformant (vendor-primary/secondary-analysis/government-primary/standards-body) and are normalised by scripts/conform_research.py

source_notes.title: Ruler — apply the same rules to all coding agents
source_notes.excerpt: Ruler centralizes AI coding assistant instructions in a single .ruler/ directory and automatically distributes them across 30+ agents (Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Aider, Gemini CLI...). MCP servers defined once in ruler.toml are propagated to native config. Backups, ruler.toml validation, safe revert.

source_notes.title: rulesync — utility CLI for AI coding agents
source_notes.excerpt: Generates configuration files for AI dev tools from unified rule files. Supports rules, ignore files, MCP, commands, subagents, skills, hooks, and permissions across 30+ platforms (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI...). Import/export + convert between tools; supports fetching official skills.

source_notes.title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills
source_notes.excerpt: We've published Agent Skills as an open standard for cross-platform portability. (December 18, 2025). Skills supported across Claude.ai, Claude Code, the Claude Agent SDK, and the Claude Developer Platform. 'malicious skills may introduce vulnerabilities... install skills only from trusted sources.'

source_notes.title: Introducing Agent Skills (open standard adopted by OpenAI)
source_notes.excerpt: In December 2025, Anthropic released the Agent Skills specification as an open standard, and OpenAI adopted the same format for Codex CLI and ChatGPT.

source_notes.title: Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit — first OSS framework covering all 10 OWASP Agentic risks
source_notes.excerpt: MIT-licensed. Addresses all 10 OWASP agentic risks. Includes SLSA-compatible build provenance, cryptographic chain from source to artifact, Dependabot scanning, pinned dependencies with cryptographic hashes, Ed25519 signing + manifest verification. MCP Security Gateway addresses tool poisoning, typosquatting, hidden instructions. Trust scoring 0-1000; four-tier execution rings.

source_notes.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 + Agentic Top 10 (late 2025)
source_notes.publisher: OWASP GenAI Security Project (via TrojAI analysis)
source_notes.excerpt: Prompt injection holds the top spot for the second edition; Supply Chain Vulnerabilities rose to #3. In late 2025 OWASP released a separate Top 10 for Agentic AI covering uncontrolled autonomy, delegated identity abuse, cross-agent prompt injection.

source_notes.title: Promptfoo vs Garak — LLM red-teaming / prompt-injection scanners
source_notes.excerpt: Garak (NVIDIA) is an OSS LLM vulnerability scanner with 37+ probes including direct/indirect/encoding-based prompt injection. Promptfoo covers 50+ vulnerability types, YAML test configs, and runs as a CI check on every deployment.

source_notes.title: AI Policy overhauled with new Impact Assessment Tool and Procurement Guidance
source_notes.excerpt: Updated Policy for responsible use of AI in government effective 15 Dec 2025; first mandatory requirement begins 15 June 2026, remaining requirements Dec 2026. New AI Impact Assessment Tool and Guidance on AI procurement.

source_notes.title: New AI technical standard to support responsible government adoption
source_notes.excerpt: The AI technical standard provides requirements/recommendations across three lifecycle phases — Discover, Operate and Retire — to embed transparency, accountability and safety from inception to decommission. Practices span the full system life cycle, including operate-phase (runtime) controls.

source_notes.title: ISM-1730 SBOM control + Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing
source_notes.excerpt: ISM-1730 requires a software bill of materials be produced and made available to consumers. ISM procurement guidance: a supply chain risk assessment is performed for suppliers; products procured from suppliers that have demonstrated a commitment to the security of their products and services.

source_notes.title: Securing the Software Supply Chain: ISM, IRAP, and the Essential Eight
source_notes.excerpt: IRAP is the framework ASD uses to evaluate ICT systems against the ISM; assessment is required prior to processing/storing government data. Essential Eight is mandatory for federal agencies to Maturity Level Two under the PSPF, with annual compliance reporting.

claims.statement: The author-once-deploy-to-many-platforms capability (Markdown source -> Claude Code/Copilot/Cursor/Codex) is fully covered by mature open-source tools (ruler, rulesync), so agent-enterprise's distribution layer is reinvention, not novelty.

evidence.quote: Ruler centralizes AI coding assistant instructions in a single .ruler/ directory and automatically distributes them across 30+ agents (Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI...).
Generates configuration files... Supports rules, ignore files, MCP, commands, subagents, skills, hooks, and permissions across 30+ platforms.

claims.falsifier: Show a concrete artefact transformation agent-enterprise performs that ruler/rulesync cannot — trust-label propagation across the include/@ref graph, capability-manifest validation, or per-target compiled approval gates — AND that bolting agent-enterprise's safety layer onto their output is materially harder than building distribution from scratch.

claims.statement: Agent Skills became an open cross-platform standard (Dec 2025) adopted by OpenAI; the platforms themselves now carry portability, so a third-party build-time resolver for skill portability is a thin wrapper the vendors have already absorbed.

evidence.quote: We've published Agent Skills as an open standard for cross-platform portability. (December 18, 2025).
In December 2025, Anthropic released the Agent Skills specification as an open standard, and OpenAI adopted the same format for Codex CLI and ChatGPT.

claims.falsifier: Demonstrate the open SKILL.md standard does NOT cover the multi-platform divergence agent-enterprise resolves (Copilot/Cursor still use incompatible native formats) and that this divergence is durable rather than converging.

claims.statement: The 'safety in consumption' core (provenance manifest, SBOM, dependency/secret scan, trust labels, capability scoping, hidden-instruction lint) is already delivered, free and MIT-licensed, by Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit — which additionally covers runtime, signing (Ed25519), SLSA provenance and all 10 OWASP agentic risks.

evidence.quote: MIT-licensed. Addresses all 10 OWASP agentic risks. Includes SLSA-compatible build provenance, cryptographic chain from source to artifact, Dependabot scanning, pinned dependencies with cryptographic hashes, Ed25519 signing + manifest verification. MCP Security Gateway addresses tool poisoning, typosquatting, hidden instructions.
In late 2025 OWASP released a separate Top 10 for Agentic AI covering uncontrolled autonomy, delegated identity abuse, cross-agent prompt injection.

claims.falsifier: Show the MS toolkit operates only on running agents / .NET-Microsoft-stack frameworks and does NOT validate static authored Markdown skill artefacts pre-deploy across Claude Code/Copilot/Cursor/Codex — i.e., agent-enterprise's build-time-artefact scope is a genuine gap it leaves open for heterogeneous vendor-neutral repos.

claims.statement: Injection-detection and prompt-injection linting are commodity: NVIDIA garak (37+ injection probes) and promptfoo (50+ vuln types, runs as a CI check) already do build/CI-time injection scanning, so agent-enterprise's unicode/bidi + injection-marker linter is reinventing existing CI scanners.

evidence.quote: Garak (NVIDIA) is an OSS LLM vulnerability scanner with 37+ probes including direct/indirect/encoding-based prompt injection. Promptfoo covers 50+ vulnerability types... runs as a CI check on every deployment.

claims.falsifier: Demonstrate garak/promptfoo test a live model's RESPONSES (dynamic red-teaming), whereas agent-enterprise statically lints authored artefact TEXT for hidden-instruction smuggling (zero-width/bidi) before any model runs — a distinct, complementary control they do not perform on static skill source.

claims.statement: agent-enterprise's supply-chain/secret/SBOM defence depends on external scanners (semgrep, grype, syft, detect-secrets) that it orchestrates but does not bundle, version-own, or guarantee — so the 'fail-closed' guarantee is only as strong as tools it neither ships nor controls, and an open decision still debates whether to degrade-gracefully when a scanner is absent.

evidence.quote: Includes... Dependabot scanning, pinned dependencies with cryptographic hashes, Ed25519 signing + manifest verification.

claims.falsifier: Show the build pins and vendors the scanner toolchain (locked versions, hermetic execution) AND fails closed when any scanner is missing — resolve the open decision in favour of hard-fail and prove it in CI.

claims.statement: The DTA AI technical standard mandates controls across Discover/Operate/Retire — including the Operate (runtime) phase — but agent-enterprise explicitly is 'not a runtime sandbox' and disclaims downstream model/agent behaviour, so as scoped it cannot satisfy operate-phase assurance and is at best a partial, pre-deployment control.

evidence.quote: The AI technical standard provides requirements/recommendations across three lifecycle phases — Discover, Operate and Retire... including operate-phase (runtime) controls.
Focus: accountability, mandatory use-case impact assessment, procurement safeguards, transparency, technical assurance.

claims.falsifier: Reframe scope honestly: position agent-enterprise as covering only the Discover/build-and-procure controls and the artefact-supply-chain slice, with an explicit documented hand-off to runtime tooling for the Operate phase — a complementary control, not end-to-end assurance.

claims.statement: For AU federal use that processes government data, IRAP assessment against the ISM is the gating expectation; agent-enterprise has no IRAP assessment, no security classification posture, and a single-maintainer bus-factor, so it cannot be procured as an assessed control regardless of its design merits.

evidence.quote: IRAP is the framework ASD uses to evaluate ICT systems against the ISM; assessment is required prior to processing/storing government data. Essential Eight is mandatory for federal agencies to Maturity Level Two under the PSPF.

ISM procurement guidance: a supply chain risk assessment is performed for suppliers; products procured from suppliers that have demonstrated a commitment to the security of their products and services.

claims.falsifier: Show agent-enterprise is a developer build-time tool that never processes or stores government data at runtime (so IRAP of the artefact pipeline is not the gating control), and that its outputs FEED an agency's existing IRAP-assessed system — i.e., it lowers the assessment burden rather than itself requiring IRAP.

claims.statement: By the project's own admission, the provenance manifest proves 'what the resolver recorded at build time, not that a downstream agent behaved correctly' — a hash proves an output was recorded, not that a command ran — so its headline tamper-evidence/provenance feature is materially weaker than the SLSA/in-toto signing other tools provide and cannot be marketed as runtime or execution assurance.

evidence.quote: SLSA-compatible build provenance, cryptographic chain from source to artifact... Ed25519 signing + manifest verification.

claims.falsifier: Ship the heavier supply-chain tier (Cosign signing + SLSA-style attestation) so provenance is cryptographically verifiable by a third party, not just an internally-recorded hash chain.

claims.statement: ISM-1730 already mandates SBOMs and ACSC provides the requirement; SBOM generation is commodity (syft/CycloneDX), so 'we emit a CycloneDX SBOM' is meeting a baseline obligation with off-the-shelf tooling, not a differentiator.

evidence.quote: ISM-1730 requires a software bill of materials be produced and made available to consumers.
pinned dependencies with cryptographic hashes... Dependabot scanning.

claims.falsifier: Show that no existing SBOM tool models the agent-artefact dependency graph (skill->@ref->imported skill->declared MCP/npm/pip dep) and that agent-enterprise's SBOM captures injection-relevant provenance (trust labels, capability surface) a stock syft SBOM omits.

claims.statement: 'Build-time shift-left for agent artefacts' is not a durable niche: frontier vendors are folding portability into open standards and shipping native permission/sandbox/governance layers, so the niche will be absorbed and a solo Markdown+Python project cannot out-iterate them.

evidence.quote: Anthropic released the Agent Skills specification as an open standard, and OpenAI adopted the same format for Codex CLI and ChatGPT.
MIT-licensed... four-tier execution rings... trust scoring 0-1000... MCP Security Gateway addresses tool poisoning, typosquatting, hidden instructions.

claims.falsifier: Show a sustained, vendor-neutral need: agencies running heterogeneous agents (Claude+Copilot+Cursor+Codex) need ONE policy-governed artefact repo with a fail-closed gate no single vendor will provide cross-platform.

claims.statement: A single-maintainer Markdown+git+Python project with no independent security assessment, no published threat-model review, and a not-yet-built safety roadmap (Sprints 3-6 still planned) fails ISM procurement expectations that suppliers demonstrate a sustained commitment to product security.

evidence.quote: products procured from suppliers that have demonstrated a commitment to the security of their products and services.

claims.falsifier: Show the safety core is shipped and tested (not roadmap), CI gates green, plus governance evidence (CODEOWNERS, security policy, documented review cadence, more than one maintainer or a sponsoring team).

claims.statement: The Essential Eight (mandatory ML2 for federal agencies) targets patching, MFA, app control, backups and admin restriction — none of which agent-enterprise addresses; it therefore contributes nothing to the agency's mandatory Essential Eight compliance posture and shouldn't be justified on that basis.

evidence.quote: Essential Eight is mandatory for federal agencies to Maturity Level Two under the PSPF, with annual compliance reporting.

claims.falsifier: This kill-claim over-reaches: agent-enterprise never claimed to be an Essential Eight control. The team rebuts by stating it targets ISM software-supply-chain controls (e.g. ISM-1730 SBOM) and DTA AI-assurance, not Essential Eight — a category error in the criticism, not a project defect.
